data.title: Liberty Latin America outlines path to pre-hurricane profitability in Jamaica by end of 2026 while advancing AI, 5G, and cost controls
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Liberty Latin America (LILA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Balan Nair, President, CEO & Director, reported "Our business performed very well in 2025. We added over 225,000 mobile postpaid subscribers across the group, notably driven by Costa Rica and supported by fixed mobile convergence efforts and continuing prepaid to postpaid migrations." He highlighted a "positive net add contribution from Puerto Rico for the first time since the migration" and noted adjusted OIBDA of $1.7 billion for full year 2025, representing 9% growth on a rebased basis despite headwinds from Hurricane Melissa.
  • The CEO described a strong recovery in profitability in Puerto Rico and double-digit adjusted OIBDA growth in Cable & Wireless Panama, with B2B registering a strong fourth quarter. He stated, "LLA registered P&E additions for the group at 14% as a percentage of revenue for full year 2025, in line with previously communicated intentions and representing a 2 percentage point decline versus the prior year."
  • Nair emphasized Jamaica's resilience post-hurricane, stating, "As we rebuild in fixed and continue our network transformation in mobile, we aim to invest in an innovative and returns-focused manner." He added that Liberty plans to "target being back close to pre-hurricane levels of profitability by the end of 2026."
  • Costa Rica's postpaid segment continues to be a standout, with over 160,000 postpaid subscribers added and strong 5G adoption. On strategic partnerships, "we are particularly excited about our recently announced partnership with AWS to bring AWS compute and AI models to our local markets for our customers."
  • Christopher Noyes, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "For Q4, we delivered revenue of $1.2 billion, reflecting 1% year-over-year rebased growth. This was fueled by double-digit top line growth at Liberty Networks and CWP, offset in large part by declines in LC, principally due to the hurricane and LPR as a result of the year-over-year decline in customers." He also said, "We reported adjusted OIBDA of $451 million in Q4, bringing our 2025 full year adjusted OIBDA to $1.7 billion."

Outlook

  • Management remains focused on rebuilding in Jamaica, expecting to "be run rating near pre-hurricane levels by year-end, which should set us up for a full recovery in 2027."
  • Continued expansion of 5G, AI-driven operational improvements, and disciplined capital investments are strategic priorities for 2026.
  • The company expects "financial performance at LLA and across our markets to be heavily weighted to the second half of the year" due to the timing of the Jamaican recovery and cost-out initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $1.2 billion, with full year 2025 revenue at $4.4 billion. Adjusted OIBDA reached $451 million in Q4 and $1.7 billion for the year, reflecting 8% and 9% rebased growth, respectively.
  • P&E additions totaled $640 million in 2025, down from $725 million in 2024. Adjusted OIBDA less P&E additions grew 27% year-over-year to $1.1 billion in 2025.
  • Adjusted free cash flow before partner distributions was $278 million in Q4, bringing the full year figure to $150 million, a 29% year-over-year increase. The company also collected $81 million in net proceeds from the weather derivatives program in Q4 to mitigate hurricane impacts.
  • Consolidated net leverage improved to 4.3x with $8.4 billion in total debt and $800 million in cash. Liberty Puerto Rico's leverage remains elevated, and management continues to pursue liability management and liquidity-raising strategies.

Q&A

  • Matthew Harrigan, StoneX: Asked about organic tailwinds versus step function growth from MANTA and El Salvador, and the impact of AI and the AWS partnership on operating expenses. Balan Nair responded that both projects offer "very good margins on it" and are "hugely accretive," but complex. He also noted, "Most of our models and most of our services and compute and storage is done over AWS... The relationship with AWS is strong for our internal usage." On AI, Nair said, "The opportunity is large. Let's be clear. I don't want to put a number on this. But clearly, as you pointed out, we have a lot of repetitive processes in our company, and we have a lot of things that perhaps we're not really good at. And AI can actually make us a lot better... we're just seeing the beginnings of it."
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: Inquired about fixed to mobile convergence (FMC) opportunity and discounting versus accretive packaging. Nair explained, "The fixed mobile convergence have been a real benefit for us... it provides 2 things. One, a higher ARPU in the home or that specific customer, so the customer ARPU goes up. And secondly, churn goes down." When pressed on revenue growth targets, Nair stated, "Our mobile product is growing because as we move from pre to post, ARPU gets better, we attach it to our fixed and we start growing. So the mobile product, you'll see growth. It won't be in the double digits, but it will be very respectable single-digit growth annually."
  • Chris Hoare, New Street Research: Asked about Puerto Rico's top line trajectory and B2B revenue. Nair cited "huge amount of improvements in business" and a "real big turnaround in our NPS scores." He said, "January, we had a very good month in January. So without any of the Boost subscribers moving up to postpaid. So we continue to see the progress in that." On B2B, Nair acknowledged a "challenge for us in 2025," but pointed to new leadership and focus for 2026.
  • Hoare also asked about timing for shareholder returns. Nair replied, "I suspect that sometime during the course of this year, we are going to come out with something that together with our Board, make some decisions that I think will reward a lot of the shareholders that's been with this."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed cautious optimism, with recurring questions on the timing of margin expansion, FMC execution, and top-line stabilization, especially in Puerto Rico and Jamaica.
  • Management tone was confident and forward-looking in prepared remarks, focusing on recovery and innovation. In Q&A, Nair used phrases such as "we feel really confident about the business" and "I think we are on our path to a great comeback," but also acknowledged challenges and the need for patience in Puerto Rico and Jamaica.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone signaled increased confidence in recovery and execution, while analysts focused more on sustainable growth drivers and cost efficiency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized recovery from Hurricane Melissa, particularly in Jamaica, and outlined a clear path to profitability restoration by end of 2026, which was not as explicit in the previous quarter.
  • Management continues to highlight FMC, AI, and 5G as strategic levers, with the AWS partnership and MANTA project receiving greater detail this quarter.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from near-term cost-cutting and operational stabilization to long-term growth potential and shareholder returns.
  • Financial results saw improvements in OIBDA margins, cash flow, and leverage, with more pronounced discussion of capital allocation and liability management in Puerto Rico.

Risks and Concerns

  • The impact and pace of recovery from Hurricane Melissa in Jamaica remains a foremost challenge, with management targeting run rate profitability by end of 2026 but acknowledging regional differences and ongoing infrastructure rebuilds.
  • Puerto Rico's competitive pressures and elevated leverage levels persist, with management actively pursuing liability management exercises and potential liquidity-raising, but no definitive decisions yet.
  • Price competition in Costa Rica's fixed segment and tough B2B comparisons are ongoing headwinds.
  • Management seeks to mitigate risks through cost reduction, capital discipline, innovation, and leveraging insurance proceeds for recovery.

Final Takeaway

Liberty Latin America enters 2026 focused on restoring profitability in Jamaica to pre-hurricane levels by year-end, expanding 5G and AI capabilities, and driving cost efficiencies across its footprint. Management points to robust subscriber growth in Costa Rica, a recovery trajectory in Puerto Rico, and disciplined capital allocation as key drivers for long-term shareholder value, while highlighting that recovery in Jamaica and enhanced cash flow delivery will be weighted toward the year's second half.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold Royalty upsizes credit facility to $150M, cuts interest rate
data.description:
• Gold Royalty (GROY) entered an agreement to amend and upsize its existing credit facility to up to US$150M.

  • The credit facility matures in November 2028.

data.title: CF Industries anticipates 9.5M tons ammonia output in 2026 as Yazoo City recovery extends
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CF Industries Holdings, Inc. (CF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Chris Bohn, CEO, opened by highlighting that CF Industries generated approximately $2.9 billion in adjusted EBITDA for the full year 2025, driven by high operational performance and industry dynamics. He stated, "These strong results reflect outstanding operational performance by the CF Industries team, the enduring advantages of our manufacturing and distribution network and constructive global nitrogen industry dynamics that have persisted into 2026."
  • Bohn noted that the Yazoo City Complex incident in November will keep the facility offline until at least Q4 2026, reducing expected gross ammonia production to approximately 9.5 million tons in 2026.
  • Progress at Blue Point, the company's joint venture with JERA and Mitsui, was discussed: "The project has progressed well from positive FID in April through hitting all our planned milestones by the end of the year... We expect to begin civil work at the Blue Point site in the second quarter of 2026." (CEO Bohn)
  • Bohn emphasized the company's capital returns: "We returned $1.7 billion to shareholders in 2025. This included deploying over $1.3 billion to repurchase 16.6 million shares, approximately 10% of the outstanding shares at the beginning of the year."
  • Bert Frost, Executive Vice President & Chief Commercial Officer, described ongoing tightness in the global nitrogen market due to delayed new capacity, supply constraints, and robust demand. He pointed to strong urea prices and significant demand from India, Brazil, North America, and Europe. He also highlighted progress with the POET partnership and growing interest in low-carbon ammonia.
  • Richard Hoker, Interim CFO, reported, "For the full year 2025, the company reported net earnings attributable to common stockholders of approximately $1.5 billion or $8.97 per diluted share. EBITDA was approximately $2.8 billion and adjusted EBITDA was approximately $2.9 billion. For the fourth quarter of 2025, we reported net earnings attributable to common stockholders of $404 million, or $2.59 per diluted share."
  • Hoker also disclosed two impairment charges totaling $76 million in Q4, including $51 million for the Donaldsonville electrolyzer pilot and $25 million related to Yazoo City. He added, "We satisfied the business interruption insurance deductible in December and expect to begin receiving insurance proceeds based on lost profitability during 2026."

Outlook

  • Management expects the Yazoo City Complex to remain offline until at least the fourth quarter of 2026, with total network ammonia production forecast at approximately 9.5 million tons in 2026.
  • Hoker stated, "We expect capital expenditures in 2026 to total approximately $1.3 billion on a consolidated basis. CF Industries portion of this is approximately $950 million, which includes $550 million for sustaining CapEx for our existing network, plus approximately $400 million relating to both the Blue Point joint venture and the common infrastructure we are building."
  • Bohn described the expectation for continued substantial free cash flow generation, citing "constructive global nitrogen industry dynamics ahead."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net earnings attributable to common stockholders were $404 million, or $2.59 per diluted share, with EBITDA at $731 million and adjusted EBITDA at $821 million.
  • Net cash from operations in 2025 was $2.75 billion, and free cash flow was approximately $1.8 billion.
  • CF completed a $1 billion senior notes offering in Q4, refinancing $750 million in debt due December 2026 and increasing financial flexibility.
  • The company repurchased 4.1 million shares for $340 million in Q4, completing its $3 billion share repurchase program and initiating a new $2 billion program, with $1.7 billion remaining authorized.
  • Two impairment charges were recognized: $51 million for the Donaldsonville electrolyzer pilot and $25 million for Yazoo City.

Q&A

  • Andrew Wong, RBC Capital Markets, asked about Blue Point project spending pace and expansion potential. Management responded that overall expenditure remains forecast at $3.7 billion, with clarified timing of cash outflows. CEO Bohn emphasized, "The site itself that we purchased could hold up to 5 ammonia plants world scale the size of this one, 1.5 million metric tons."
  • Joel Jackson, BMO Capital Markets, questioned CBAM impacts and Yazoo City recovery. CEO Bohn stated CBAM is in place and premiums for low-carbon products are being achieved, with upside not modeled in project returns. For Yazoo City, Bohn said, "Our intent is to get the plant up as soon as possible. The time frame that we've given with late Q4 here in 2026 is just as we've gone out to get switchgear and some of the electrical stuff, which is longer lead time items, we've been given those dates as delivery."
  • Benjamin Theurer, Barclays, asked about market tightness and 2026 EBITDA drivers. Frost described supply limitations and demand strength across major markets, noting urea pricing at $450 a short ton in NOLA, $100 higher than December 2025.
  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo, pressed on CBAM and Blue Point pricing. Frost responded that premiums are in place for 2026 contracts and broader global demand for low-carbon products continues.
  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer, asked about low-carbon fertilizer demand and operating costs. Frost highlighted the POET partnership and growing demand, while Bohn anticipated BI insurance would offset most Yazoo City-related costs.
  • Lucas Beaumont, UBS, inquired about the sustainability of pricing premiums over the cost curve and energy cost convergence. Bohn expects gas price differentials to persist but stressed supply-demand tightness will support pricing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts probed recurring topics of project timelines, market tightness, premium pricing, and supply risks, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone. They pressed for details on insurance recovery and CBAM impacts but did not display overt skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing operational strength and strategic progress, with phrases such as "We are confident..." and "We expect..." appearing frequently. In responses to challenging questions, management remained direct, providing detailed context and acknowledging uncertainties where appropriate.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained upbeat, while analysts continued to focus on supply constraints and market premiums.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 now reflects a reduction in gross ammonia production to 9.5 million tons due to the Yazoo City incident, compared to prior expectations of 10 million tons.
  • Management continues to highlight constructive nitrogen market conditions and robust free cash flow, similar to the previous quarter, but now places more emphasis on the timing and insurance recovery related to Yazoo City.
  • Strategic focus remains on Blue Point and low-carbon ammonia initiatives, with increased commentary on insurance proceeds and impairment charges this quarter.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted more to project execution timelines and insurance recovery, away from broader strategic and valuation concerns of the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • The Yazoo City Complex incident remains a key challenge, with management stating production will not resume until Q4 2026 at the earliest.
  • Impairment charges related to the Yazoo City incident and the Donaldsonville electrolyzer pilot underscore execution and return-on-investment risks.
  • Geopolitical risks, supply constraints, and CBAM policy uncertainty were discussed as ongoing market risks.
  • Management highlighted business interruption insurance as a mitigation strategy for Yazoo City’s lost profitability, expecting proceeds during 2026.

Final Takeaway

CF Industries underscored its ability to generate substantial free cash flow and return capital to shareholders, even while navigating the significant impact of the Yazoo City outage and advancing major growth initiatives like Blue Point. The company expects the global nitrogen market to remain tight, with robust demand and supply constraints shaping a constructive outlook for 2026. Management’s confidence in operational execution and strategic positioning was consistently reinforced throughout the call, with ongoing emphasis on shareholder value creation and the company’s readiness to capitalize on market and low-carbon product opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bank of America commits $25B to private credit deals - report
data.description:
Bank of America (BAC) is putting up $25B of its cash for private credit deals, following other Wall Street peers that are tapping their balance sheets to lend to the fast-growing market, according to a media report.

The bank will put its own capital into private credit opportunities, expanding its existing direct-lending business, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. Bank of America (BAC) expects to originate the deals from its capital markets unit, part of its investment banking division, they said.

The company named Anand Melvani as head of private credit within the capital markets unit, according to a memo that Bloomberg has reviewed. Melvani, who has been at BofA  for over 30 years, will also keep his role as head of Americas leveraged finance.

According to a separate memo cited by Bloomberg, Scott Wiate will be head of private credit, structuring, and underwriting. He was most recently head of enterprise credit risk for global leveraged finance, global credit, and municipal banking and markets.

data.title: Occidental targets 1.45M boe/d production in 2026 with $5.5B-$5.9B capital plan as cost efficiencies deepen
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vicki Hollub, President and CEO, described 2025 as an exceptional year, emphasizing that "the sale of OxyChem made possible by the quality of our portfolio was a deliberate step to strengthen our balance sheet and enable us to deliver greater value from our high-return oil and gas assets." Hollub noted a new annual production record of 1.4 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, surpassing guidance while spending $300 million less in oil and gas capital than planned and reducing annual operating expenses by $275 million. She highlighted the completion of a 10-year portfolio transformation, stating, "we no longer require transformative acquisitions. Instead, our teams are focused on what they do best, and that is execution, including cost reduction, capital efficiency and well performance."
  • Sunil Mathew, CFO, stated, "In the fourth quarter, we delivered strong operational and financial results. We generated an adjusted profit of $0.31 per diluted share and a reported loss of ($0.07) per diluted share. The difference was largely driven by charges and transaction costs related to the sale of OxyChem." He highlighted free cash flow generation of approximately $1 billion despite lower realized oil prices and emphasized the successful reduction of principal debt to $15 billion, with a $700 million tender offer announced to further reduce debt to $14.3 billion.
  • Richard Jackson, COO, emphasized operational achievements, including record production, a 15% reduction in new well capital costs in U.S. onshore, and ongoing cost efficiency efforts that delivered approximately $2 billion in annual oil and gas cost savings since 2023. He noted that "these efficiencies further enabled us to reduce U.S. onshore capital by $400 million compared to 2025 while still delivering a 1% production growth."
  • Hollub announced that Jordan Tanner will transition to a leadership role in the Gulf of America, with Babatunde Cole appointed as Vice President of Investor Relations.

Outlook

  • Hollub outlined 2026 priorities: maintaining production base through safe operations, sustaining and growing dividends (including an 8% increase to the quarterly dividend), and further strengthening the financial position with continued net debt reductions. She said, "We expect capital spending to range from $5.5 billion to $5.9 billion, representing a $550 million reduction from 2025, excluding OxyChem."
  • Mathew projected, "we expect production to average approximately 1.45 million barrels of oil equivalent per day," and said, "we expect to improve free cash flow by more than $1.2 billion in 2026."
  • The company expects approximately 70% of oil and gas capital to be allocated to U.S. onshore operations, with flexibility to respond to commodity price improvements.

Financial Results

  • Occidental generated $4.3 billion in free cash flow before working capital in 2025, even as oil prices declined by around 14% from 2024.
  • On a normalized basis and excluding OxyChem, cash flow from operations increased by 27% year-over-year.
  • Principal debt reduced to $15 billion, with a tender offer expected to bring this to $14.3 billion.
  • The Midstream segment exceeded the midpoint of guidance by more than $500 million in adjusted pretax income for the year; Q4 adjusted pretax income exceeded guidance by $172 million.
  • The company achieved its lowest lease operating expense per barrel of oil equivalent since 2021, with Q4 domestic operating expense at $7.77 per BOE.

Q&A

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: Asked about the lower CapEx guide and the drivers of the $800 million reduction. Hollub stated, "our teams came up with these ideas on what we should do and what were the best projects. But as they continue to optimize those projects, it was pretty amazing to see the cost that they were able to cut out, the efficiencies that they were able to find."
  • Jayaram also asked about the Horn Mountain waterflood project and its potential to support a sustaining production profile in the Gulf. Dillon responded, "this waterflood, the King dump flood, the future waterfloods... will move from a 20% to sub-10% decline by 2030 and improving to below 5% in subsequent years."
  • Nitin Kumar, Mizuho: Inquired about the unconventional portion of the sub-$30 per barrel breakeven resource. Hollub explained, "the secondary benches are providing now as much value as the primary benches did... the average resource is about a $38 per barrel breakeven."
  • Kumar followed up on share buybacks. Mathew said, "we would like to first get our principal debt to $10 billion, but we are not setting a time frame to get to this target as we want to have some flexibility."
  • Wei Jiang, Barclays: Explored the sustainability of cost savings into 2027. Mathew noted, "we expect to maintain this momentum into next year. So we could see a modest growth with this year's capital depending on the efficiency and new well performance."
  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: Asked about sustaining capital at different oil prices and the outlook for LCV. Mathew outlined how sustaining capital is calculated, "from $5.7 billion, you back out LCV and exploration of $300 million, you're at $5.4 billion... and going from $5.2 billion to $4.1 billion at $40, that is primarily deflation, around 20% deflation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on CapEx reductions, cost efficiency sustainability, capital allocation flexibility, and sustaining production levels, with a generally positive but probing tone. Multiple questions centered on structural cost savings and their impact on future years.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, using phrases like "we are perfectly positioned" and "I'm confident that our teams will continue to innovate." In Q&A, management continued to emphasize operational excellence and cost discipline, reiterating flexibility and the sustainability of improvements.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but placed greater emphasis on cost discipline and flexibility, while analysts maintained a constructive but slightly more detailed and probing approach regarding the durability of efficiencies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 capital spending was notably reduced to $5.5 billion–$5.9 billion from the previous "soft guide" range of $6.3 billion–$6.7 billion, reflecting deeper cost efficiencies and project optimization.
  • Dividend growth focus strengthened with the announcement of an 8% increase.
  • Debt reduction accelerated, with the principal debt moving to $15 billion and a new target of $14.3 billion.
  • Analysts in both quarters questioned the sustainability of cost reductions and the impact of capital allocation, but current quarter questions showed greater scrutiny on the structural nature of savings and the long-term outlook for sustaining capital.
  • Management confidence increased regarding the resource base and operational agility, highlighting a shift from transformative acquisitions to execution and efficiency as the core strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the ongoing industry challenge of reserves replacement and the importance of maintaining a high reserves replacement ratio.
  • The outlook for oil prices remains uncertain, with Hollub expressing caution about the sustainability of recent geopolitical price spikes.
  • Analysts raised questions about the durability of cost savings, the ability to sustain production growth, and the flexibility of capital allocation in different macro scenarios.
  • Mathew acknowledged that no timeframe is set for reaching the $10 billion principal debt target, signaling market-dependent flexibility.

Final Takeaway

Occidental Petroleum delivered a year of record operational and financial performance, marked by substantial cost efficiencies, balance sheet strengthening, and a strategic focus on sustaining and growing shareholder returns. The company’s 2026 plan projects lower capital spending of $5.5 billion to $5.9 billion while targeting average production of 1.45 million BOE per day. Management emphasized continued cost discipline, resilient free cash flow growth, and capital allocation flexibility, positioning Oxy for long-term value creation amid uncertain commodity markets and industry-wide challenges in reserves replacement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Upbound Group outlines $4.7B–$4.95B 2026 revenue target while advancing digital growth and Brigit integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Upbound Group, Inc. (UPBD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Fahmi Karam, CEO, highlighted 2025 as a year of “significant progress” for Upbound, noting the company served over 3.5 million customers and expanded its business through the acquisition of Brigit. He emphasized Upbound’s transformation into a digital and data-driven platform for underserved consumers, stating, “Our team's dedication and shared vision have driven key achievements that we believe strongly position Upbound for continued success and long-term growth.”
  • The addition of Brigit, a subscription-based financial health technology company, was described as a milestone, with Karam underscoring its “remarkable growth and delivered significant value to its users.” He cited the pilot of a new line of credit offering and cross-selling initiatives as key developments.
  • Two executive appointments were announced: Hal Khouri as CFO and Rebecca Wooters as Chief Growth Officer. Karam described Wooters’ role as leading “digital transformation and initiatives and implement data-driven solutions across all 3 major segments of the company.”
  • Karam reported that revenue grew 8.7% to approximately $4.7 billion, and non-GAAP diluted EPS reached $4.13, near the high end of guidance. He added, “Our cash flow generation was particularly strong in 2025 with free cash flow of $180 million, increasing over $130 million year-over-year.”
  • Hal Khouri, CFO, stated, “Acima revenue grew 8.6% year-over-year, which was its ninth consecutive quarter of revenue growth and adjusted EBITDA of $87 million was up 7.3% from a year ago.” He also noted, “Brigit finished the quarter with approximately 1.6 million paid subscribers, which was a nearly 30% increase from the year ago period.”

Outlook

  • Upbound projects 2026 consolidated revenue in the range of $4.7 billion to $4.95 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $500 million to $535 million, and fully diluted non-GAAP EPS of $4 to $4.35. Free cash flow is expected to increase to approximately $200 million.
  • Acima’s 2026 GMV and revenue are expected to increase mid-single digits year-over-year, with adjusted EBITDA margin in line with 2025 and losses “stabilizing in the 9.5% area for the year.”
  • Brigit is expected to deliver annualized revenue growth of over 30% in the $265 million to $285 million range and adjusted EBITDA in the $50 million to $60 million range. Management cited delays in new product rollouts and macro uncertainty as impacting the variance from initial estimates.
  • Rent-A-Center’s 2026 revenue is expected to be flat to positive relative to 2025, with adjusted EBITDA margins remaining stable.

Financial Results

  • 2025 revenue reached approximately $4.7 billion, the highest in company history. Adjusted EBITDA was nearly $510 million.
  • Fourth quarter consolidated revenue was $1.2 billion, a 10.9% increase year-over-year, with the Brigit segment and Acima’s 8.6% growth as primary drivers.
  • Non-GAAP diluted EPS for Q4 was $1.01. Free cash flow for the year was $180 million, and liquidity at year-end stood at $358 million.
  • Acima’s fourth quarter GMV was nearly $550 million, with a loss rate of 10.1% in line with prior guidance. Brigit recorded $64.6 million of Q4 revenue and a 17.2% adjusted EBITDA margin.
  • Rent-A-Center delivered nearly $480 million in Q4 revenue, with same-store sales growth of 80 basis points and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 14.4%.

Q&A

  • Robert Griffin, Raymond James, asked about the seasonality in EPS guidance. CEO Karam explained, “you have a full benefit of a full quarter of Brigit being in the numbers. So... you only had 2/3 of the quarter with Brigit on our books.”
  • Griffin inquired about Brigit’s guidance and integration. Karam responded that delays in new product launches and macro uncertainty were key factors, adding, “We want to be very cautious... around our loss profile and kind of judging where the consumer is.” He described integration as “light touch” so far, with more substantial integration planned for late 2026 into 2027.
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG, asked about Acima’s GMV cadence. Karam stated, “the first half of the year will be relatively flat... and then we kind of get back to a seamless norm as we lap some of the changes that we made in the second half of the year.”
  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens, asked about tax refund impacts. Karam indicated a normal tax season was assumed, with higher refunds potentially benefiting revenue but possibly lowering gross profit margin.
  • Hoang Nguyen, TD Cowen, inquired about Brigit competition and revenue cadence. Karam said the competitive landscape was intensifying but emphasized differentiation through product and feature enhancements, expecting subscriber growth to be “back-end loaded.”
  • Yuna Sohn, Jefferies, asked about margin trends. CFO Khouri explained, “the competitive landscape... is putting pressure across the board on margins,” with higher costs and tariffs impacting profitability.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was probing and focused on clarifying guidance shape, product integration, and competitive pressures, with repeated questions on Brigit’s outlook and Acima’s credit tightening.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks, shifting to a more cautious stance in Q&A, especially regarding Brigit’s delayed rollout and macro uncertainties. Phrases such as “We want to be very cautious” and “a combination of things that has caused us to slow down” indicated measured optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s sentiment shifted from emphasizing innovation and growth to a more balanced message of opportunity and risk management. Analysts’ tone remained consistently diligent, pressing for specifics on growth drivers and risk factors.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 consolidated revenue and non-GAAP EPS is largely consistent with previous projections but reflects more cautious assumptions for Brigit’s growth, noting “delays in new products” and “macro uncertainty.”
  • The previous quarter highlighted the onboarding of new executives and strong Brigit momentum, while the current quarter focuses on execution and integration challenges.
  • Analysts in both quarters emphasized clarity on underwriting tightening and the impact on GMV and losses, but current questions intensified around Brigit’s product delays and the competitive environment.
  • Management’s tone evolved from highlighting aggressive innovation to expressing disciplined growth and integration pacing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that the “core consumer continues to navigate a challenging environment, including the cumulative effects of inflation and elevated prices for essentials like groceries, rent and utilities, which weigh on their purchasing power.”
  • Both Acima and Rent-A-Center implemented “targeted risk management adjustments” in response to evolving macro conditions.
  • Brigit’s outlook was tempered by “delays in new product rollouts” and “macro uncertainty,” leading to conservative guidance.
  • Legal and regulatory matters remain, with $72 million accrued for settlements, primarily relating to two ongoing cases.
  • Analysts raised concerns over margin pressure, competitive threats in Brigit’s space, and the sustainability of recent improvements in loss rates and GMV growth.

Final Takeaway

Upbound Group’s 2025 performance reflected strong execution on digital transformation and portfolio diversification, with the Brigit acquisition and executive team expansion playing central roles. Looking ahead, the company projects continued growth and stable profitability in 2026, but with a more measured approach to product launches and integration, particularly at Brigit. Management remains focused on operational excellence, disciplined credit risk, and leveraging data-driven insights to drive cross-segment synergies while navigating ongoing macro pressures and regulatory challenges.
